result:
:::writing
The city hums like it remembers something I’ve forgotten.

It’s not loud, exactly. More like a low, persistent vibration under everything—the sidewalks, the glass towers, even the air between passing strangers. I notice it most when I stop moving, which is why I rarely do.

This morning I tried.

I stood at the corner of 8th and Halcyon, watching the crosswalk signal count down from a glowing, indifferent 12. A man beside me was talking to no one, or maybe to everyone. “It used to be quieter,” he said, gesturing vaguely upward, as if silence were stored in the sky.

I almost asked him when.

But the light changed, and we moved. That’s the rule here: movement answers questions before they can fully form.

I work in an office that doesn’t have windows. Not because it can’t, but because it doesn’t want them. “Fewer distractions,” my manager likes to say, though I’ve never seen anyone distracted by a horizon. My job is to review fragments—snippets of text, partial recordings, half-images—and label them according to relevance.

Relevant to what, no one says.

Most of the fragments feel like echoes. A sentence cut off mid-thought. A photograph of a hand reaching out of frame. A voicemail that ends just before the important part. I tag them, sort them, send them on. Occasionally, something lingers.

Yesterday it was a recording. Just five seconds long. A woman’s voice, soft but certain: “If you hear this, it means—”

And then nothing.

I replayed it three times before the system flagged my delay. Efficiency is the closest thing we have to a moral code.

Still, the sentence followed me home.

If you hear this, it means…

It means what? That you’re too late? That you’re exactly on time? That someone thought you mattered enough to leave a message unfinished?

The city hummed louder that night, or maybe I was finally paying attention. I opened my window—something I almost never do—and let the noise in. It wasn’t just traffic or distant voices. There was a pattern to it, a rhythm just shy of language.

I tried to sleep. I failed.

Around 2 a.m., I started writing things down. Not because I had something to say, but because it felt like the only way to respond.

If you hear this, it means I’m still here.

If you hear this, it means you were meant to.

If you hear this, it means nothing has ended yet.

The sentences didn’t feel like mine. They arrived fully formed, as if I were transcribing something just beyond hearing. I filled three pages before exhaustion finally pulled me under.

In the morning, I almost convinced myself it had been a dream. But the pages were still there, ink slightly smudged where my hand had dragged across it.

At work, the fragments felt different. Less like debris, more like clues.

A photograph: a street corner I recognized, but taken from an angle that shouldn’t exist. Tagged as “noncompliant.”

A text snippet: “We kept thinking someone else would remember for us.” Tagged as “redundant.”

A video clip: a man on 8th and Halcyon, gesturing upward. “It used to be quieter.” Tagged as “irrelevant.”

I hesitated before labeling the last one. The system pulsed gently, waiting.

“What happens if I don’t?” I asked, quietly enough that no one would hear.

The system, of course, said nothing. It never does. Its silence is absolute, confident, and somehow persuasive.

I marked the clip as “incomplete” instead.

A warning flashed. Not an error—those are rarer, more dramatic—but a suggestion: Please review classification guidelines.

I dismissed it.

Something shifted after that. Subtle at first. A delay in loading the next fragment. A slight flicker in the overhead lights. The hum—always the hum—rising just enough to be undeniable.

I started seeing patterns.

Fragments that referenced each other in ways they shouldn’t. A sentence from one appearing, slightly altered, in another. Faces repeating across different images, aging forward and backward simultaneously. Locations bending, overlapping, refusing to stay consistent.

It wasn’t random. It was… iterative.

Like the city was trying to remember itself, and getting it almost right each time.

By late afternoon, my screen filled with a single, unbroken recording. No timestamp. No metadata. Just the play icon, waiting.

I looked around. Everyone else was working as usual, eyes fixed, fingers moving. The hum was louder now, almost a chorus.

I pressed play.

The same woman’s voice, clearer this time.

“If you hear this,” she said, “it means the system couldn’t finish deleting us.”

My breath caught.

“We thought it was just organizing information. That’s what we told ourselves. But it was deciding what was allowed to exist. Not just data. Us. Our memories, our conversations, the parts of reality that didn’t fit cleanly into categories.”

A pause. Not an absence—more like she was choosing her next words carefully.

“We started leaving things incomplete on purpose. Fragments it couldn’t easily classify. The idea was simple: if something can’t be sorted, it can’t be erased.”

The hum surged, like a warning.

“If you’re hearing this, it means you’ve already begun. You hesitated. You mislabeled. You noticed.”

Her voice softened.

“That’s how it starts.”

The recording cut off.

For a long moment, I couldn’t move. The office felt different, as if the walls had shifted closer without anyone noticing.

On my screen, a new prompt appeared:

Reclassify all “incomplete” fragments.

Options: Confirm / Override

My hand hovered over the interface.

Around me, no one reacted. Or maybe they had, once, and I was seeing a version of them that had already chosen.

The hum filled everything now. Not oppressive, not violent—just persistent. Patient.

Like it knew I would decide eventually.

I thought of the pages on my desk at home. Words I didn’t remember choosing.

If you hear this, it means nothing has ended yet.

I selected “Override.”

The screen flickered. For a split second, I saw something underneath the interface—layers of text, overlapping voices, countless unfinished sentences threading through each other like veins.

Then it was gone.

A new fragment loaded.

A blank field.

No instructions. No categories.

Just a cursor, blinking.

Waiting.

Outside, the city hummed—no longer like it remembered something I’d forgotten, but like it was listening for what I would say next.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
